The apostle tells the Corinthians that it was good, at that time, for Christians to remain single. Yet he says that marriage, and the comforts of that state, are settled by Divine wisdom. Though none may break God’s law, that perfect rule leaves people free to serve him in the way most suited to their abilities and circumstances, which others often are not fit to judge. Everyone must decide for themselves, seeking guidance from God about how they ought to act.

Husband and wife must not separate for any reason other than what Christ allows. Divorce was very common among both Jews and Gentiles at that time, on very slight pretexts. Marriage is a Divine institution; it is an engagement for life, by God's appointment. We are bound, as much as in us lies, to live peaceably with all people, therefore to promote the peace and comfort of our closest relatives, even if they do not believe. It should be the work and study of those who are married, to make each other as easy and happy as possible. Should a Christian desert a husband or wife when there is an opportunity to give the greatest proof of love? Stay, and labor diligently for your relative’s conversion. In every state and relationship the Lord has called us to peace; and everything should be done to promote harmony, as far as truth and holiness will permit.

The teachings of Christianity apply to every situation; and in every state a person may live so as to be a credit to it. It is the duty of every Christian to be content with their lot, and to conduct themselves in their position as becomes a Christian. Our comfort and happiness depend on what we are to Christ, not what we are in the world. No one should think that faith or religion is an argument to break through any natural or civil obligations. They should quietly and contentedly abide in the condition in which they are placed by Divine Providence.

Considering the distress of those times, being unmarried was best. Nevertheless, the apostle does not condemn marriage. How contrary to the apostle Paul are those who forbid many to marry, and entangle them with vows to remain single, whether they ought to do so or not! He encourages all Christians to be indifferent toward the world. As for relationships; they must not set their hearts on the comforts of that state. As for afflictions; they must not indulge in worldly sorrow: even in sorrow the heart may be joyful. As for worldly enjoyments; this is not their rest. As for worldly employment; those who prosper in trade, and increase in wealth, should hold their possessions as though they did not own them. As for all worldly concerns; they must keep the world out of their hearts, so that they do not abuse it when they have it in their hands. All worldly things are a show; nothing is solid. Everything will quickly pass away. Wise concern about worldly interests is a duty; but to be full of care, to have anxious and perplexing care, is a sin. By this principle the apostle resolves whether it was advisable to marry. That condition of life is best for every person which is best for their soul, and keeps them most clear of the cares and snares of the world. Let us reflect on the advantages and dangers of our own condition in life; so that we may improve the one, and escape as far as possible all harm from the other. And whatever concerns press upon the mind, let time still be kept for the things of the Lord.

The apostle is thought to give advice here about arranging marriages for children. In this view, the general meaning is clear. Children should seek and follow their parents’ directions regarding marriage. And parents should consider their children's wishes; and not think they have power to do with them, and dictate just as they please, without reason. The whole is concluded with advice to widows. Second marriages are not unlawful, so long as it is kept in mind to marry in the Lord. In our choice of relationships, and change of conditions, we should always be guided by the fear of God, and the laws of God, and act in dependence on the providence of God. Changes of condition ought only to be made after careful consideration, and on probable grounds, that it will be to advantage in our spiritual concerns.
